commands.name: ls
commands.description: List directory contents

patterns.text: list all files in the current directory
patterns.explanation: Lists files and directories in the current working directory

patterns.text: show hidden files and dotfiles
patterns.explanation: The -a flag includes entries starting with a dot

patterns.text: list files with details like size and permissions
patterns.explanation: Long format with human-readable file sizes

patterns.text: sort files by modification time newest first
patterns.explanation: Long listing sorted by modification time, most recent first

patterns.text: show only directories in current path
patterns.explanation: Lists only directories by matching the trailing slash pattern

commands.name: cp
commands.description: Copy files and directories

patterns.text: copy a file to another location
patterns.explanation: Creates a duplicate of the source file at the destination path

patterns.text: copy a folder and everything inside it recursively
patterns.explanation: The -r flag copies directories and their contents recursively

patterns.text: copy a file but don't overwrite if destination exists
patterns.explanation: The -n flag prevents overwriting existing files

patterns.text: copy files preserving permissions and timestamps
patterns.explanation: The -p flag preserves mode, ownership, and timestamps

patterns.text: copy multiple files into a directory
patterns.explanation: Copies multiple source files into the target directory

commands.name: mv
commands.description: Move or rename files and directories

patterns.text: rename a file
patterns.explanation: Renames a file by moving it to a new name in the same directory

patterns.text: move a file into a different directory
patterns.explanation: Relocates the file to the specified directory

patterns.text: move a file but ask before overwriting
patterns.explanation: The -i flag prompts before overwriting an existing file

patterns.text: rename a directory
patterns.explanation: Renames a directory by moving it to a new name

patterns.text: move all text files to a folder
patterns.explanation: Uses a glob pattern to move all .txt files into the target directory

commands.name: rm
commands.description: Remove files or directories

patterns.text: delete a file
patterns.explanation: Permanently removes the specified file

patterns.text: delete a directory and all its contents
patterns.explanation: Recursively and forcefully removes a directory and everything in it

patterns.text: remove a file with confirmation prompt
patterns.explanation: The -i flag asks for confirmation before deleting

patterns.text: remove all log files in the current directory
patterns.explanation: Uses a glob pattern to delete all files ending in .log

patterns.text: delete an empty directory
patterns.explanation: The -d flag removes empty directories

commands.name: mkdir
commands.description: Create directories

patterns.text: create a new folder
patterns.explanation: Creates a single directory with the given name

patterns.text: create nested directories including parent folders that don't exist
patterns.explanation: The -p flag creates intermediate parent directories as needed

patterns.text: create a directory with specific permissions
patterns.explanation: The -m flag sets the directory permissions on creation

patterns.text: create multiple directories at once
patterns.explanation: Creates several directories in a single command

patterns.text: make a full directory tree structure in one command
patterns.explanation: Uses brace expansion with -p to create a project directory structure

commands.name: rmdir
commands.description: Remove empty directories

patterns.text: remove an empty directory
patterns.explanation: Deletes a directory only if it contains no files

patterns.text: remove nested empty directories from the bottom up
patterns.explanation: Removes c, then b, then a if each becomes empty after the child is removed

patterns.text: delete an empty folder without error if it doesn't exist
patterns.explanation: Suppresses errors when the directory is not empty

patterns.text: clean up a chain of empty parent directories
patterns.explanation: Removes each directory component if it is empty after its child is removed

patterns.text: remove multiple empty directories at once
patterns.explanation: Removes several empty directories in a single command

commands.name: touch
commands.description: Create empty files or update file timestamps

patterns.text: create an empty file
patterns.explanation: Creates the file if it does not exist; updates its timestamp if it does

patterns.text: update the modification time of a file to now
patterns.explanation: Sets the access and modification times to the current time

patterns.text: create multiple empty files at once
patterns.explanation: Creates several empty files in a single command

patterns.text: set a specific timestamp on a file
patterns.explanation: Sets the timestamp to January 15, 2023 at 12:00 using -t

patterns.text: make a file's timestamp match another file
patterns.explanation: Copies the timestamp from the reference file to the target file

commands.name: ln
commands.description: Create hard and symbolic links

patterns.text: create a symbolic link to a file
patterns.explanation: Creates a symlink that points to the original file

patterns.text: create a symlink to a directory
patterns.explanation: Creates a symbolic link pointing to a directory

patterns.text: create a hard link to a file
patterns.explanation: Creates a hard link sharing the same inode as the original

patterns.text: overwrite an existing symlink with a new target
patterns.explanation: The -f flag forces replacement of the existing link

patterns.text: create a symbolic link with a relative path
patterns.explanation: Creates a relative symlink pointing to a file in a parent directory

commands.name: stat
commands.description: Display detailed file or filesystem status

patterns.text: show detailed metadata about a file
patterns.explanation: Displays size, permissions, inode, timestamps, and other metadata

patterns.text: get just the file size in bytes
patterns.explanation: Uses the format flag to output only the file size

patterns.text: check the inode number of a file
patterns.explanation: Prints the inode number using format specifier

patterns.text: display when a file was last modified
patterns.explanation: Shows the last modification time as a Unix timestamp

patterns.text: show the permissions of a file in octal
patterns.explanation: Displays the file permissions as an octal number

commands.name: file
commands.description: Determine file type

patterns.text: find out what type a file is
patterns.explanation: Inspects file contents to determine its type (text, binary, image, etc.)

patterns.text: check the mime type of a file
patterns.explanation: Outputs the MIME type instead of the human-readable description

patterns.text: identify file types for everything in a directory
patterns.explanation: Runs file type detection on every file in the current directory

patterns.text: check if a file is a binary or text file
patterns.explanation: Reports whether the file contains ASCII text, binary data, etc.

patterns.text: get the encoding of a text file
patterns.explanation: Outputs the character encoding (utf-8, ascii, etc.)

commands.name: basename
commands.description: Strip directory and suffix from filenames

patterns.text: get just the filename from a full path
patterns.explanation: Strips the directory path and returns only report.pdf

patterns.text: extract the filename without its extension
patterns.explanation: Removes the directory path and the specified suffix

patterns.text: strip the directory from a path in a script
patterns.explanation: Returns the script name without the directory path

patterns.text: get the last component of a directory path
patterns.explanation: Returns bin, the last component of the path

patterns.text: remove the .txt extension from a filename
patterns.explanation: Strips the .txt suffix and returns just report

commands.name: dirname
commands.description: Strip the last component from a file path

patterns.text: get the directory part of a file path
patterns.explanation: Returns /home/user/documents, stripping the filename

patterns.text: find the parent directory of a file
patterns.explanation: Outputs /var/log as the parent directory

patterns.text: get the directory the current script is in
patterns.explanation: Returns the directory containing the running script

patterns.text: extract the folder path from a full filepath
patterns.explanation: Returns /tmp/data without the filename

patterns.text: get the directory two levels up from a file
patterns.explanation: Nesting dirname calls strips two levels, returning /a/b

commands.name: realpath
commands.description: Resolve the absolute path, resolving symlinks

patterns.text: get the absolute path of a file resolving all symlinks
patterns.explanation: Returns the canonical absolute path with all symlinks resolved

patterns.text: resolve a relative path to its full absolute path
patterns.explanation: Converts a relative path to an absolute canonical path

patterns.text: find the real location a symlink points to
patterns.explanation: Follows the symlink chain and returns the final resolved path

patterns.text: get the canonical path without requiring the file to exist
patterns.explanation: The -m flag resolves the path even if components do not exist

patterns.text: get the relative path from one directory to another
patterns.explanation: Computes the relative path from the first directory to the second

commands.name: pwd
commands.description: Print the current working directory

patterns.text: show what directory I am currently in
patterns.explanation: Prints the absolute path of the current working directory

patterns.text: print the physical current directory without symlinks
patterns.explanation: Resolves symlinks and shows the actual physical directory

patterns.text: get my current location in the filesystem
patterns.explanation: Outputs the full path of where you are

patterns.text: display the working directory including symlink path
patterns.explanation: Shows the logical path, preserving symlink names

commands.name: cd
commands.description: Change the current working directory

patterns.text: go to my home directory
patterns.explanation: Changes to the user's home directory

patterns.text: go up one directory level
patterns.explanation: Navigates to the parent directory

patterns.text: go back to the previous directory
patterns.explanation: Returns to the last directory you were in

patterns.text: navigate to a specific folder
patterns.explanation: Changes the working directory to the specified path

patterns.text: change to the root directory
patterns.explanation: Navigates to the filesystem root

commands.name: tree
commands.description: Display directory structure as a tree

patterns.text: show the directory structure as a tree
patterns.explanation: Recursively displays the current directory in a tree-like format

patterns.text: show only directories in the tree, no files
patterns.explanation: The -d flag limits output to directories only

patterns.text: display a tree limited to 2 levels deep
patterns.explanation: The -L flag limits the recursion depth

patterns.text: show the tree with file sizes
patterns.explanation: Displays each file's size in human-readable format

patterns.text: show directory tree but ignore node_modules
patterns.explanation: The -I flag excludes directories matching the given pattern

commands.name: du
commands.description: Estimate file and directory space usage

patterns.text: check how much space a directory takes up
patterns.explanation: Summarizes total size in human-readable format

patterns.text: find the biggest files and folders in the current directory
patterns.explanation: Shows sizes of top-level items sorted by size, top 10

patterns.text: show disk usage of all subdirectories one level deep
patterns.explanation: Lists sizes for immediate subdirectories only

patterns.text: get the total size of a folder in megabytes
patterns.explanation: Summarizes total size in megabytes

patterns.text: see how much disk space each subfolder uses
patterns.explanation: Shows subdirectory sizes sorted from largest to smallest

commands.name: df
commands.description: Report filesystem disk space usage

patterns.text: check free disk space on all mounted filesystems
patterns.explanation: Shows disk space usage in human-readable format for all mounts

patterns.text: see how much space is left on the root partition
patterns.explanation: Shows disk usage for the filesystem containing the root directory

patterns.text: display filesystem types alongside disk usage
patterns.explanation: Includes the filesystem type column in the output

patterns.text: show disk usage in gigabytes
patterns.explanation: Displays sizes in gigabyte units

patterns.text: check how much disk space a specific mount point has
patterns.explanation: Shows usage statistics for the filesystem containing /home

commands.name: mount
commands.description: Mount a filesystem

patterns.text: list all currently mounted filesystems
patterns.explanation: Without arguments, displays all current mounts

patterns.text: mount a USB drive to a directory
patterns.explanation: Attaches the filesystem on the device to the mount point

patterns.text: mount an ISO image file
patterns.explanation: Uses a loop device to mount an ISO file as a filesystem

patterns.text: mount a filesystem as read-only
patterns.explanation: The ro option mounts the filesystem in read-only mode

patterns.text: remount a filesystem with different options
patterns.explanation: Changes mount options without unmounting and remounting

commands.name: umount
commands.description: Unmount a filesystem

patterns.text: unmount a USB drive
patterns.explanation: Detaches the filesystem at the given mount point

patterns.text: safely eject a mounted disk
patterns.explanation: Unmounts the filesystem on the specified device

patterns.text: force unmount a busy filesystem
patterns.explanation: The -f flag forces the unmount even if the filesystem is busy

patterns.text: lazy unmount a filesystem that is in use
patterns.explanation: Detaches the filesystem immediately and cleans up references lazily

patterns.text: unmount all filesystems of a specific type
patterns.explanation: The -a flag unmounts all and -t filters by filesystem type

commands.name: mktemp
commands.description: Create a temporary file or directory safely

patterns.text: create a temporary file and get its path
patterns.explanation: Creates a unique temporary file in /tmp and prints its path

patterns.text: create a temporary directory
patterns.explanation: The -d flag creates a temporary directory instead of a file

patterns.text: create a temp file with a custom name pattern
patterns.explanation: Uses a template where X characters are replaced with random values

patterns.text: make a temporary file with a specific suffix
patterns.explanation: Creates a temp file ending with the specified suffix

patterns.text: create a named temporary file in the current directory
patterns.explanation: The -p flag specifies the directory for the temporary file

commands.name: install
commands.description: Copy files and set attributes in one step

patterns.text: copy a binary and set it executable in one step
patterns.explanation: Copies the file and sets permissions to rwxr-xr-x

patterns.text: install a file with specific owner and group
patterns.explanation: Copies the file setting owner, group, and permissions

patterns.text: create a directory with specific permissions using install
patterns.explanation: The -d flag creates all components of the specified directories

patterns.text: copy a script making it executable and owned by root
patterns.explanation: Installs the script with root ownership and executable permissions

patterns.text: install a config file with backup of the original
patterns.explanation: The -b flag creates a backup of the destination file before overwriting

commands.name: shred
commands.description: Securely overwrite a file to hide its contents before deleting

patterns.text: securely delete a sensitive file so it can't be recovered
patterns.explanation: Overwrites with random data, zeros, then removes the file; -v shows progress

patterns.text: wipe a file with multiple overwrite passes
patterns.explanation: Overwrites the file 10 times with random data

patterns.text: overwrite a disk device to destroy all data
patterns.explanation: Overwrites the entire block device with random data

patterns.text: securely erase a file and remove it afterward
patterns.explanation: The -u flag truncates and removes the file after overwriting

patterns.text: overwrite a file with zeros instead of random data before deleting
patterns.explanation: Uses -n 0 to skip random passes, -z for a single zero pass, then removes

commands.name: truncate
commands.description: Shrink or extend the size of a file

patterns.text: empty a file without deleting it
patterns.explanation: Sets the file size to 0 bytes, effectively clearing its contents

patterns.text: create a file of a specific size for testing
patterns.explanation: Creates a sparse file that appears to be 1 gigabyte

patterns.text: shrink a file to a specific size
patterns.explanation: Truncates or extends the file to exactly 100 megabytes

patterns.text: reduce a file size by a certain amount
patterns.explanation: The minus sign reduces the file size by 10 megabytes

patterns.text: extend a file by appending empty space
patterns.explanation: The plus sign grows the file by 50 megabytes, padded with null bytes

commands.name: fallocate
commands.description: Preallocate or deallocate space for a file

patterns.text: preallocate a file of a specific size quickly
patterns.explanation: Instantly allocates 10 GB of disk space without writing data

patterns.text: create a swap file of a given size
patterns.explanation: Allocates space for a 4 GB swap file

patterns.text: punch a hole in a file to free up space in the middle
patterns.explanation: Deallocates space within the file without changing its apparent size

patterns.text: quickly create a large test file
patterns.explanation: Allocates 500 MB almost instantly without writing zeros

commands.name: sync
commands.description: Flush filesystem caches to disk

patterns.text: flush all pending writes to disk
patterns.explanation: Forces all buffered data to be written to the underlying storage

patterns.text: sync a specific file's data to disk
patterns.explanation: Flushes only the specified file's data to disk

patterns.text: ensure all data is written before safely removing a drive
patterns.explanation: Writes all cached data ensuring safe hardware removal

patterns.text: flush just the file data without metadata
patterns.explanation: Syncs only the data, skipping unnecessary metadata updates

patterns.text: force write filesystem buffers before shutdown
patterns.explanation: Double sync is a traditional safety measure to ensure all data is flushed

commands.name: chroot
commands.description: Run a command with a different root directory

patterns.text: change the root directory and run a shell inside it
patterns.explanation: Starts a bash shell with /mnt/newroot as the apparent root filesystem

patterns.text: repair a broken linux install by chrooting into it
patterns.explanation: Changes root to the mounted broken system for repair operations

patterns.text: run a command inside a chroot environment
patterns.explanation: Executes a clean shell in the chroot jail

patterns.text: isolate a process in a sandboxed root filesystem
patterns.explanation: Runs the server with filesystem access limited to /sandbox

patterns.text: run a command as a different user inside a chroot
patterns.explanation: Changes to the specified user and group before running the command in the chroot

commands.name: dd
commands.description: Convert and copy a file with low-level control

patterns.text: create a bootable USB drive from an ISO image
patterns.command: sudo dd if=image.iso of=/dev/sdb bs=4M status=progress
patterns.explanation: Writes the ISO byte-for-byte to the USB device with progress display

patterns.text: clone an entire disk to another disk
patterns.command: sudo dd if=/dev/sda of=/dev/sdb bs=64K status=progress
patterns.explanation: Copies every byte from one disk to another

patterns.text: create a disk image backup of a partition
patterns.command: sudo dd if=/dev/sda1 of=backup.img bs=4M status=progress
patterns.explanation: Creates a raw image file of the partition

patterns.text: write zeros to a disk to erase it
patterns.command: sudo dd if=/dev/zero of=/dev/sdb bs=1M status=progress
patterns.explanation: Overwrites the entire disk with zeros

patterns.text: generate a file filled with random data
patterns.explanation: Creates a 100 MB file of random bytes

commands.name: rsync
commands.description: Efficiently sync files and directories locally or remotely

patterns.text: sync a directory to a backup location
patterns.explanation: Archive mode sync with verbose output and human-readable sizes

patterns.text: copy files to a remote server over SSH
patterns.explanation: Syncs to a remote server with compression enabled

patterns.text: sync files and delete anything in destination not in source
patterns.explanation: Mirrors source exactly, removing extra files from destination

patterns.text: resume an interrupted large file transfer
patterns.explanation: Keeps partial transfers and shows progress for resumable copy

patterns.text: sync only files that are newer than the destination
patterns.explanation: The -u flag skips files that are newer on the destination

patterns.text: do a dry run to see what rsync would change without actually copying
patterns.explanation: The -n flag performs a trial run with no changes made

commands.name: rename
commands.description: Rename multiple files using a pattern

patterns.text: rename files by replacing a string in their names
patterns.explanation: Replaces the first occurrence of 'old' with 'new' in matching filenames

patterns.text: change the extension of multiple files
patterns.explanation: Renames all .txt files to .md using a regex substitution

patterns.text: convert filenames to lowercase
patterns.explanation: Transliterates uppercase characters to lowercase in all filenames

patterns.text: add a prefix to all files in a directory
patterns.explanation: Prepends 'prefix_' to every filename

patterns.text: do a dry run to preview what rename would change
patterns.explanation: The -n flag shows what would be renamed without actually doing it

commands.name: readlink
commands.description: Print the target of a symbolic link

patterns.text: see where a symlink points
patterns.explanation: Prints the link target path on one line

patterns.text: resolve a symlink to its ultimate canonical path (GNU readlink)
patterns.explanation: -f follows every symlink component to the final file

patterns.text: show one level of symlink target on macOS using stat
patterns.explanation: Prints where the symlink points; use realpath or repeated reads for full canonicalization

commands.name: inotifywait
commands.description: Wait for changes to files using inotify

patterns.text: watch a directory for new files and print their names
patterns.explanation: Runs continuously and outputs events as files are created

patterns.text: wait until a specific file is modified
patterns.explanation: Blocks until a modification event occurs on the file

commands.name: fswatch
commands.description: A cross-platform file change monitor

patterns.text: watch a directory and run a command when files change
patterns.explanation: Triggers the script execution upon any filesystem event in the directory

commands.name: dirs
commands.description: Display list of currently remembered directories

patterns.text: show the directory stack managed by pushd and popd
patterns.explanation: Prints the directory stack with index numbers
